text: Hello, I'm sharing a recent RTAP for asymptomatic inguinal hernia. There was a little bit of an interesting lipoma, if that's what you want to call it, because I couldn't really tell if it was lipoma or nodal tissue or both, but it looked like it had a separate hernia defect with it. So that's the main reason why I'm sharing this video.
The surgeon begins creating a flap from the anterior superior iliac spine (ASIS) to the medial umbilical ligaments, followed by a medial dissection.
The surgeon identifies the bone structure and begins lateral dissection adjacent to it, carefully separating tissue layers.
The surgeon sweeps cremasteric tissue laterally, revealing the chord structures and the vas deferens in the surgical field.
The surgeon reduces the hernia sac, which includes some preperitoneal fat, and proceeds to parietalize the cord structures, highlighting anatomical features during the dissection.
The surgeon exposes the femoral defect and continues dissection down to the outer hyperspace, revealing the anatomical structures in this area.
The surgeon continues to parietalize the cord structures, ensuring the mesh can sit flat against the sidewall without clamshelling. Further parietalization is performed down to the psoas muscle.
The surgeon demonstrates the anatomy of the myopectineal orifice and searches for a cord lipoma. Fatty tissue is observed extending into the inguinal canal, with the iliopubic tract visible nearby.
The surgeon identifies herniated fat entering a separate defect lateral to the internal ring and removes the tissue. As the dissection continues, nodal tissue over the iliac vessels becomes visible.
The surgeon encounters increased bleeding during dissection and decides to halt further dissection, leaving the tissue in place after reducing the herniated fat.
The surgeon places a 16x12 mesh over the myopectineal orifice to cover the defect. Once the mesh is positioned correctly, the flap is closed to secure the mesh in place.

These cases are taking me about forty-five minutes these days, which I'm very pleased with. I usually do not use a Foley. You see that this patient had a Foley at the start of the procedure, and that's because when the camera was first put in, despite having had the patient voided prior to the start of the case, the bladder was very, very distended and was precluded in the safety section, so I have a Foley placed in trial. The majority of my patients will go home same day. I do not usually ask them to void before they go home, and I do not send my patients home with lymphatics.
And that's the case. I hope you enjoyed.
